tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

raw.description: Whether to return without compression (in PNG format). Default is false, which returns a JPEG image.

filename.description: File name to save the screenshot to. Defaults to `page-{timestamp}.{png|jpeg}` if not specified.

element.description: Human-readable element description used to obtain permission to screenshot the element. If not provided, the screenshot will be taken of viewport. If element is provided, ref must be provided too.

ref.description: Exact target element reference from the page snapshot. If not provided, the screenshot will be taken of viewport. If ref is provided, element must be provided too.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You are able to connect and use a virtual browser with persistent session.
  Browser tools are provided via MCP.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Scrape effect blog at https://effect.website/blog and find the content of last 3 articles.
          Create/update Person object for each author.
          Create Markdown document for each article.

response.delta: Let me start by navigating to the Effect blog to scrape the articles
, and also check what schemas are available for Person objects.
by navigating to the Effect blog and checking the available schemas for Person objects.

tools.name: browser_pdf_save
tools.description: Save page as PDF

filename.description: File name to save the pdf to. Defaults to `page-{timestamp}.pdf` if not specified.

tools.name: browser_take_screenshot
tools.description: Take a screenshot of the current page. You can't perform actions based on the screenshot, use browser_snapshot for actions.

raw.description: Whether to return without compression (in PNG format). Default is false, which returns a JPEG image.

filename.description: File name to save the screenshot to. Defaults to `page-{timestamp}.{png|jpeg}` if not specified.

element.description: Human-readable element description used to obtain permission to screenshot the element. If not provided, the screenshot will be taken of viewport. If element is provided, ref must be provided too.

ref.description: Exact target element reference from the page snapshot. If not provided, the screenshot will be taken of viewport. If ref is provided, element must be provided too.

tools.name: browser_snapshot
tools.description: Capture accessibility snapshot of the current page, this is better than screenshot

tools.name: browser_click
tools.description: Perform click on a web page

element.description: Human-readable element description used to obtain permission to interact with the element

ref.description: Exact target element reference from the page snapshot

doubleClick.description: Whether to perform a double click instead of a single click

tools.name: browser_drag
tools.description: Perform drag and drop between two elements

startElement.description: Human-readable source element description used to obtain the permission to interact with the element

startRef.description: Exact source element reference from the page snapshot

endElement.description: Human-readable target element description used to obtain the permission to interact with the element

endRef.description: Exact target element reference from the page snapshot

tools.name: browser_hover
tools.description: Hover over element on page

element.description: Human-readable element description used to obtain permission to interact with the element

ref.description: Exact target element reference from the page snapshot

tools.name: browser_type
tools.description: Type text into editable element

element.description: Human-readable element description used to obtain permission to interact with the element

ref.description: Exact target element reference from the page snapshot

text.description: Text to type into the element

submit.description: Whether to submit entered text (press Enter after)

slowly.description: Whether to type one character at a time. Useful for triggering key handlers in the page. By default entire text is filled in at once.

tools.name: browser_select_option
tools.description: Select an option in a dropdown

element.description: Human-readable element description used to obtain permission to interact with the element

ref.description: Exact target element reference from the page snapshot

values.description: Array of values to select in the dropdown. This can be a single value or multiple values.

tools.name: browser_tab_list
tools.description: List browser tabs

tools.name: browser_tab_new
tools.description: Open a new tab

url.description: The URL to navigate to in the new tab. If not provided, the new tab will be blank.

tools.name: browser_tab_select
tools.description: Select a tab by index

index.description: The index of the tab to select

tools.name: browser_tab_close
tools.description: Close a tab

index.description: The index of the tab to close. Closes current tab if not provided.

tools.name: browser_generate_playwright_test
tools.description: Generate a Playwright test for given scenario

tools.name: browser_wait_for
tools.description: Wait for text to appear or disappear or a specified time to pass

time.description: The time to wait in seconds

textGone.description: The text to wait for to disappear

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You are able to connect and use a virtual browser with persistent session.
  Browser tools are provided via MCP.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Scrape effect blog at https://effect.website/blog and find the content of last 3 articles.
          Create/update Person object for each author.
          Create Markdown document for each article.

content.text: Let me start by navigating to the Effect blog to scrape the articles, and also check what schemas are available for Person objects.
I'll start by navigating to the Effect blog and checking the available schemas for Person objects.

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

doc.description:
The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:
- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

tools.name: update
tools.description: Applies a set of edits to the markdown document.

- DXN (dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4). DXNs can be prepended with an @ symbol for compatibility with in-text references.
- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

tools.name: create-object
tools.description:
Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You are able to connect and use a virtual browser with persistent session.
  Browser tools are provided via MCP.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Scrape effect blog at https://effect.website/blog and find the content of last 3 articles.
          Create/update Person object for each author.
          Create Markdown document for each article.

content.text: Let me start by navigating to the Effect blog to scrape the articles, and also check what schemas are available for Person objects.
I'll start by navigating to the Effect blog and checking the available schemas for Person objects.

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You are able to connect and use a virtual browser with persistent session.
  Browser tools are provided via MCP.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Scrape effect blog at https://effect.website/blog and find the content of last 3 articles.
          Create/update Person object for each author.
          Create Markdown document for each article.

      - main [ref=e208]:
        - heading "The One Weird Git Trick That Makes Coding Agents More Effect-ive" [level=1] [ref=e211]
        - generic [ref=e213]:
          - generic [ref=e214]:
            - generic [ref=e215]:
              - time [ref=e217]: May 11, 2026
              - link "Maxwell Brown Maxwell Brown Founding Engineer" [ref=e219] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://github.com/IMax153
                - img "Maxwell Brown" [ref=e220] [cursor=pointer]
                - generic [ref=e221] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - generic [ref=e222] [cursor=pointer]: Maxwell Brown
                  - generic [ref=e223] [cursor=pointer]: Founding Engineer
            - paragraph [ref=e224]: One of the most common questions we get on the Effect team in the era of AI-assisted coding is how to get coding agents to write good, idiomatic Effect code.
            - paragraph [ref=e225]: "Our answer is always the same: give your agent real library code to learn from. More specifically, give the agent access to the Effect source code."
            - generic [ref=e226]:
              - heading "Stop Making Agents Guess" [level=2] [ref=e227]
              - link "Stop Making Agents Guess" [ref=e228] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#stop-making-agents-guess"
                - img [ref=e230] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e232]:
              - text: "If you follow us on social media, you’ve probably heard us recommend cloning the Effect repository directly into your project. The advice sounds strange until you remember what coding agents are actually good at: reading and exploring"
              - emphasis [ref=e233]: code
              - text: (crazy, I know).
            - paragraph [ref=e234]:
              - text: They’re substantially
              - emphasis [ref=e235]: less
              - text: "effective when working from documentation written for humans. When source code is available, an agent can explore it the way it was designed to: by following usage patterns, tracing abstractions, and learning from existing patterns. More importantly, this approach isn’t specific to Effect - it works for any external software your application depends on."
            - paragraph [ref=e236]: The most practical method for making external software explorable by your coding agent is to simply vendor the project’s Git repository directly into your application’s codebase.
            - paragraph [ref=e237]:
              - text: In this post we will explain why this works, why
              - code [ref=e238]: git subtrees
              - text: are a good fit for it, and how to set it up without making your project harder to manage.
            - generic [ref=e239]:
              - heading "Why Having the Source Code Available Matters" [level=2] [ref=e240]
              - link "Why Having the Source Code Available Matters" [ref=e241] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#why-having-the-source-code-available-matters"
                - img [ref=e243] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e245]: A common approach when utilizing coding agents is to rely on web search / web fetch to allow the agent to retrieve information about external dependencies when required. In practice, however, the agent will only have access to isolated code snippets without the surrounding structure, often requiring it to repeatedly fetch code it has already seen or until it has enough information to proceed. This is token inefficient and leads to highly fragmented context.
            - paragraph [ref=e246]:
              - text: Documentation has a different limitation - it usually explains what an API or library
              - emphasis [ref=e247]: does
              - text: but not how it is actually
              - emphasis [ref=e248]: used
              - text: across a codebase. Coding agents rely on patterns and examples, not descriptions.
            - paragraph [ref=e249]: Having source code available locally allows us to remedy both issues at once. When the agent is unsure about how to use a particular API or library, it can explore the actual implementation of that codebase just like any other part of your project.
            - paragraph [ref=e250]:
              - text: This is also why relying on code in
              - code [ref=e251]: node_modules
              - text: usually isn’t enough. The code is often compiled or flattened, which removes the structure that makes it useful to read. Even if a particular library does publish their uncompiled source, most coding agents are deoptimized from exploring
              - code [ref=e252]: node_modules
              - text: or other gitignore directories in the first place.
            - generic [ref=e253]:
              - heading "Why Git Subtrees Instead of Git Submodules?" [level=2] [ref=e254]
              - link "Why Git Subtrees Instead of Git Submodules?" [ref=e255] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#why-git-subtrees-instead-of-git-submodules"
                - img [ref=e257] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e259]:
              - text: It might seem intuitive to simply use
              - code [ref=e260]: git submodule
              - text: to vendor external software into a project. Submodules are (unfortunately) a familiar feature of
              - code [ref=e261]: git
              - text: for most developers, keep the repositories cleanly separated, and allow pinning external codebases to a specific commit.
            - paragraph [ref=e262]:
              - text: And to be honest, submodules are
              - emphasis [ref=e263]: fine
              - text: "- they accomplish the end goal which is allowing the agent direct access to external source code. But in our opinion there are features of"
              - code [ref=e264]: git submodule
              - text: that make them quite annoying for this specific purpose.
            - list [ref=e265]:
              - listitem [ref=e266]: They require explicit initialization when cloning a project, which (if you are like me) can be really easy to forget to do
              - listitem [ref=e267]: They also introduce a layer of indirection (your repo now “points” to another repo instead of just containing the source code)
              - listitem [ref=e268]:
                - text: They require additional metadata to be tracked in git (i.e. the
                - code [ref=e269]: .gitmodules
                - text: file)
            - paragraph [ref=e270]:
              - text: Instead,
              - code [ref=e271]: git subtree
              - text: allows you to directly nest one repository inside another as a subdirectory. Once a subtree is created, it behaves like any other directory in your project. That means both the engineers and coding agents working on a project can ignore the fact that a subtree is even in use.

              - text: Ok, now you’ve got a few external repositories sitting in your

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You are able to connect and use a virtual browser with persistent session.
  Browser tools are provided via MCP.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Scrape effect blog at https://effect.website/blog and find the content of last 3 articles.
          Create/update Person object for each author.
          Create Markdown document for each article.

                - generic [ref=e221] [cursor=pointer]:

                  - generic [ref=e223] [cursor=pointer]: Founding Engineer
            - paragraph [ref=e224]: One of the most common questions we get on the Effect team in the era of AI-assisted coding is how to get coding agents to write good, idiomatic Effect code.
            - paragraph [ref=e225]: "Our answer is always the same: give your agent real library code to learn from. More specifically, give the agent access to the Effect source code."

              - text: "effective when working from documentation written for humans. When source code is available, an agent can explore it the way it was designed to: by following usage patterns, tracing abstractions, and learning from existing patterns. More importantly, this approach isn’t specific to Effect - it works for any external software your application depends on."
            - paragraph [ref=e236]: The most practical method for making external software explorable by your coding agent is to simply vendor the project’s Git repository directly into your application’s codebase.
            - paragraph [ref=e237]:

              - code [ref=e238]: git subtrees
              - text: are a good fit for it, and how to set it up without making your project harder to manage.
            - generic [ref=e239]:

            - paragraph [ref=e245]: A common approach when utilizing coding agents is to rely on web search / web fetch to allow the agent to retrieve information about external dependencies when required. In practice, however, the agent will only have access to isolated code snippets without the surrounding structure, often requiring it to repeatedly fetch code it has already seen or until it has enough information to proceed. This is token inefficient and leads to highly fragmented context.
            - paragraph [ref=e246]:
              - text: Documentation has a different limitation - it usually explains what an API or library
              - emphasis [ref=e247]: does
              - text: but not how it is actually
              - emphasis [ref=e248]: used
              - text: across a codebase. Coding agents rely on patterns and examples, not descriptions.
            - paragraph [ref=e249]: Having source code available locally allows us to remedy both issues at once. When the agent is unsure about how to use a particular API or library, it can explore the actual implementation of that codebase just like any other part of your project.

              - code [ref=e252]: node_modules

            - paragraph [ref=e234]: Hi Effecters!
            - paragraph [ref=e235]:
              - text: Welcome back to This Week In Effect (
              - strong [ref=e236]: TWIE
              - text: ) - your weekly update of the latest developments in the Effect community and ecosystem.
            - paragraph [ref=e237]: Effect is a powerful TypeScript library that helps developers build complex, synchronous, and asynchronous programs. One key feature that sets Effect apart is how it leverages structured concurrency to provide features such as async cancellation and safe resource management, making it easier to build robust, scalable, and efficient programs.
            - paragraph [ref=e238]: To get started, below you’ll find links to our documentation as well as our guide for installing Effect. Enjoy!
            - list [ref=e239]:
              - listitem [ref=e240]:
                - link "Effect Documentation" [ref=e241] [cursor=pointer]:

              - strong [ref=e245]: "Recent major updates:"

            - paragraph [ref=e270]:
              - text: A steady week for the v4 beta, with new testing utilities, schema additions, and a handful of targeted bug fixes across HTTP, observability, and RPC — here are the most notable changes that landed in
              - code [ref=e271]: effect-smol
              - text: .
            - list [ref=e272]:
              - listitem [ref=e273]:
                - paragraph [ref=e274]:
                  - strong [ref=e275]: New APIs
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e276]: HttpApiTest
                  - text: module for testing HttpApi handlers without a running server, added
                  - code [ref=e277]: Effect.acquireDisposable
                  - text: for integrating with the TC39 Explicit Resource Management proposal (
                  - code [ref=e278]: using
                  - text: keyword), and ported
                  - code [ref=e279]: Effect.firstSuccessOf
                  - text: from v3.
              - listitem [ref=e280]:
                - paragraph [ref=e281]:
                  - strong [ref=e282]: Schema additions
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e283]: Schema.DurationFromString
                  - text: for parsing duration strings, and added
                  - code [ref=e284]: Config.literals
                  - text: as a convenience constructor for
                  - code [ref=e285]: Schema.Literals
                  - text: .
              - listitem [ref=e286]:
                - paragraph [ref=e287]:
                  - strong [ref=e288]: RPC
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e289]: Rpc.custom
                  - text: for defining custom RPC handlers with full control over the request/response lifecycle.
              - listitem [ref=e290]:
                - paragraph [ref=e291]:
                  - strong [ref=e292]: HTTP
                  - text: ": Fixed empty body decoding in"
                  - code [ref=e293]: HttpApiBuilder
                  - text: ", and fixed OpenAI config fields being incorrectly spread into the request body."
              - listitem [ref=e294]:
                - paragraph [ref=e295]:
                  - strong [ref=e296]: Observability & runtime
                  - text: ": Fixed OpenTelemetry logger severity mapping, moved the fiber lifetime metric start hook to the correct lifecycle point, and fixed"
                  - code [ref=e297]: formatJson
                  - text: circular reference handling while removing a duplicated
                  - code [ref=e298]: stringifyCircular
                  - text: utility.
              - listitem [ref=e299]:
                - paragraph [ref=e300]:
                  - strong [ref=e301]: Schema error messages
                  - text: ": Normalized"
                  - code [ref=e302]: SchemaTransformation
                  - text: decoding error messages for more consistent output.
              - listitem [ref=e303]:
                - paragraph [ref=e304]:
                  - strong [ref=e305]: Platform
                  - text: ": Allowed"
                  - code [ref=e306]: "null"
                  - text: for Kubernetes condition
                  - code [ref=e307]: lastTransitionTime
                  - text: to handle optional fields in K8s manifests.
            - paragraph [ref=e308]:
              - text: You can follow the full changelog directly in the
              - link "effect-smol repository" [ref=e309] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://github.com/Effect-TS/effect-smol
              - text: .

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You are able to connect and use a virtual browser with persistent session.
  Browser tools are provided via MCP.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Scrape effect blog at https://effect.website/blog and find the content of last 3 articles.
          Create/update Person object for each author.
          Create Markdown document for each article.

content.text: Let me start by navigating to the Effect blog to scrape the articles, and also check what schemas are available for Person objects.
I'll start by navigating to the Effect blog and checking the available schemas for Person objects.

            - paragraph [ref=e224]: One of the most common questions we get on the Effect team in the era of AI-assisted coding is how to get coding agents to write good, idiomatic Effect code.
            - paragraph [ref=e225]: "Our answer is always the same: give your agent real library code to learn from. More specifically, give the agent access to the Effect source code."
            - generic [ref=e226]:
              - heading "Stop Making Agents Guess" [level=2] [ref=e227]
              - link "Stop Making Agents Guess" [ref=e228] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#stop-making-agents-guess"
                - img [ref=e230] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e232]:
              - text: "If you follow us on social media, you’ve probably heard us recommend cloning the Effect repository directly into your project. The advice sounds strange until you remember what coding agents are actually good at: reading and exploring"
              - emphasis [ref=e233]: code
              - text: (crazy, I know).
            - paragraph [ref=e234]:
              - text: They’re substantially
              - emphasis [ref=e235]: less
              - text: "effective when working from documentation written for humans. When source code is available, an agent can explore it the way it was designed to: by following usage patterns, tracing abstractions, and learning from existing patterns. More importantly, this approach isn’t specific to Effect - it works for any external software your application depends on."
            - paragraph [ref=e236]: The most practical method for making external software explorable by your coding agent is to simply vendor the project’s Git repository directly into your application’s codebase.
            - paragraph [ref=e237]:
              - text: In this post we will explain why this works, why

            - paragraph [ref=e234]: Hi Effecters!
            - paragraph [ref=e235]:
              - text: Welcome back to This Week In Effect (
              - strong [ref=e236]: TWIE
              - text: ) - your weekly update of the latest developments in the Effect community and ecosystem.
            - paragraph [ref=e237]: Effect is a powerful TypeScript library that helps developers build complex, synchronous, and asynchronous programs. One key feature that sets Effect apart is how it leverages structured concurrency to provide features such as async cancellation and safe resource management, making it easier to build robust, scalable, and efficient programs.
            - paragraph [ref=e238]: To get started, below you’ll find links to our documentation as well as our guide for installing Effect. Enjoy!
            - list [ref=e239]:
              - listitem [ref=e240]:

            - paragraph [ref=e270]:
              - text: A steady week for the v4 beta, with new testing utilities, schema additions, and a handful of targeted bug fixes across HTTP, observability, and RPC — here are the most notable changes that landed in
              - code [ref=e271]: effect-smol
              - text: .
            - list [ref=e272]:
              - listitem [ref=e273]:
                - paragraph [ref=e274]:
                  - strong [ref=e275]: New APIs
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e276]: HttpApiTest
                  - text: module for testing HttpApi handlers without a running server, added
                  - code [ref=e277]: Effect.acquireDisposable
                  - text: for integrating with the TC39 Explicit Resource Management proposal (
                  - code [ref=e278]: using
                  - text: keyword), and ported
                  - code [ref=e279]: Effect.firstSuccessOf
                  - text: from v3.
              - listitem [ref=e280]:
                - paragraph [ref=e281]:
                  - strong [ref=e282]: Schema additions
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e283]: Schema.DurationFromString
                  - text: for parsing duration strings, and added
                  - code [ref=e284]: Config.literals
                  - text: as a convenience constructor for
                  - code [ref=e285]: Schema.Literals
                  - text: .
              - listitem [ref=e286]:
                - paragraph [ref=e287]:
                  - strong [ref=e288]: RPC
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e289]: Rpc.custom
                  - text: for defining custom RPC handlers with full control over the request/response lifecycle.
              - listitem [ref=e290]:
                - paragraph [ref=e291]:
                  - strong [ref=e292]: HTTP
                  - text: ": Fixed empty body decoding in"
                  - code [ref=e293]: HttpApiBuilder
                  - text: ", and fixed OpenAI config fields being incorrectly spread into the request body."
              - listitem [ref=e294]:
                - paragraph [ref=e295]:
                  - strong [ref=e296]: Observability & runtime
                  - text: ": Fixed OpenTelemetry logger severity mapping, moved the fiber lifetime metric start hook to the correct lifecycle point, and fixed"
                  - code [ref=e297]: formatJson
                  - text: circular reference handling while removing a duplicated
                  - code [ref=e298]: stringifyCircular
                  - text: utility.
              - listitem [ref=e299]:
                - paragraph [ref=e300]:
                  - strong [ref=e301]: Schema error messages
                  - text: ": Normalized"
                  - code [ref=e302]: SchemaTransformation
                  - text: decoding error messages for more consistent output.
              - listitem [ref=e303]:
                - paragraph [ref=e304]:
                  - strong [ref=e305]: Platform
                  - text: ": Allowed"
                  - code [ref=e306]: "null"
                  - text: for Kubernetes condition
                  - code [ref=e307]: lastTransitionTime
                  - text: to handle optional fields in K8s manifests.

              - link "effect-smol repository" [ref=e309] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://github.com/Effect-TS/effect-smol

            - paragraph [ref=e231]: Hi Effecters!
            - paragraph [ref=e232]:
              - text: Welcome back to This Week In Effect (
              - strong [ref=e233]: TWIE
              - text: ) - your weekly update of the latest developments in the Effect community and ecosystem.
            - paragraph [ref=e234]: Effect is a powerful TypeScript library that helps developers build complex, synchronous, and asynchronous programs. One key feature that sets Effect apart is how it leverages structured concurrency to provide features such as async cancellation and safe resource management, making it easier to build robust, scalable, and efficient programs.
            - paragraph [ref=e235]: To get started, below you’ll find links to our documentation as well as our guide for installing Effect. Enjoy!
            - list [ref=e236]:
              - listitem [ref=e237]:
                - link "Effect Documentation" [ref=e238] [cursor=pointer]:

                  - /url: /docs/getting-started/installation/

                - text: Release.

                - link "@effect/workflow" [ref=e249] [cursor=pointer]:

            - paragraph [ref=e267]:
              - text: Here are the most notable changes that landed in
              - code [ref=e268]: effect-smol

            - list [ref=e269]:
              - listitem [ref=e270]:
                - paragraph [ref=e271]:
                  - strong [ref=e272]: Reactivity
                  - text: ": Added an"
                  - code [ref=e273]: .exhaustive()
                  - text: finalizer to the
                  - code [ref=e274]: AsyncResult
                  - text: builder, ensuring all async result states are handled before the builder is sealed.
              - listitem [ref=e275]:
                - paragraph [ref=e276]:
                  - strong [ref=e277]: OpenAI & AI
                  - text: ": Added OpenAI reasoning support, expanding the AI integration capabilities introduced in recent weeks."
              - listitem [ref=e278]:
                - paragraph [ref=e279]:
                  - strong [ref=e280]: RPC & Entity
                  - text: ": Fixed the entity proxy RPC handler context, resolving an issue where the wrong context could be injected when using entity proxies."
              - listitem [ref=e281]:
                - paragraph [ref=e282]:
                  - strong [ref=e283]: IndexedDB
                  - text: ": Cleaned up"
                  - code [ref=e284]: IndexedDb
                  - text: prototypes, continuing the refinement of the IDB module.
              - listitem [ref=e285]:
                - paragraph [ref=e286]:
                  - strong [ref=e287]: Platform & internals
                  - text: ": Allowed"
                  - code [ref=e288]: Duration.Input
                  - text: with accessors for more ergonomic duration handling, and optimized binary array generation from streams to reduce unnecessary copying.

## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You are able to connect and use a virtual browser with persistent session.
  Browser tools are provided via MCP.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Scrape effect blog at https://effect.website/blog and find the content of last 3 articles.

      - main [ref=e208]:
        - heading "The One Weird Git Trick That Makes Coding Agents More Effect-ive" [level=1] [ref=e211]
        - generic [ref=e213]:
          - generic [ref=e214]:
            - generic [ref=e215]:
              - time [ref=e217]: May 11, 2026
              - link "Maxwell Brown Maxwell Brown Founding Engineer" [ref=e219] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: https://github.com/IMax153
                - img "Maxwell Brown" [ref=e220] [cursor=pointer]
                - generic [ref=e221] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - generic [ref=e222] [cursor=pointer]: Maxwell Brown
                  - generic [ref=e223] [cursor=pointer]: Founding Engineer
            - paragraph [ref=e224]: One of the most common questions we get on the Effect team in the era of AI-assisted coding is how to get coding agents to write good, idiomatic Effect code.
            - paragraph [ref=e225]: "Our answer is always the same: give your agent real library code to learn from. More specifically, give the agent access to the Effect source code."
            - generic [ref=e226]:
              - heading "Stop Making Agents Guess" [level=2] [ref=e227]
              - link "Stop Making Agents Guess" [ref=e228] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#stop-making-agents-guess"
                - img [ref=e230] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e232]:
              - text: "If you follow us on social media, you’ve probably heard us recommend cloning the Effect repository directly into your project. The advice sounds strange until you remember what coding agents are actually good at: reading and exploring"
              - emphasis [ref=e233]: code
              - text: (crazy, I know).
            - paragraph [ref=e234]:
              - text: They’re substantially
              - emphasis [ref=e235]: less
              - text: "effective when working from documentation written for humans. When source code is available, an agent can explore it the way it was designed to: by following usage patterns, tracing abstractions, and learning from existing patterns. More importantly, this approach isn’t specific to Effect - it works for any external software your application depends on."
            - paragraph [ref=e236]: The most practical method for making external software explorable by your coding agent is to simply vendor the project’s Git repository directly into your application’s codebase.
            - paragraph [ref=e237]:
              - text: In this post we will explain why this works, why
              - code [ref=e238]: git subtrees
              - text: are a good fit for it, and how to set it up without making your project harder to manage.
            - generic [ref=e239]:
              - heading "Why Having the Source Code Available Matters" [level=2] [ref=e240]
              - link "Why Having the Source Code Available Matters" [ref=e241] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#why-having-the-source-code-available-matters"
                - img [ref=e243] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e245]: A common approach when utilizing coding agents is to rely on web search / web fetch to allow the agent to retrieve information about external dependencies when required. In practice, however, the agent will only have access to isolated code snippets without the surrounding structure, often requiring it to repeatedly fetch code it has already seen or until it has enough information to proceed. This is token inefficient and leads to highly fragmented context.
            - paragraph [ref=e246]:
              - text: Documentation has a different limitation - it usually explains what an API or library
              - emphasis [ref=e247]: does
              - text: but not how it is actually
              - emphasis [ref=e248]: used
              - text: across a codebase. Coding agents rely on patterns and examples, not descriptions.
            - paragraph [ref=e249]: Having source code available locally allows us to remedy both issues at once. When the agent is unsure about how to use a particular API or library, it can explore the actual implementation of that codebase just like any other part of your project.
            - paragraph [ref=e250]:
              - text: This is also why relying on code in
              - code [ref=e251]: node_modules
              - text: usually isn’t enough. The code is often compiled or flattened, which removes the structure that makes it useful to read. Even if a particular library does publish their uncompiled source, most coding agents are deoptimized from exploring
              - code [ref=e252]: node_modules
              - text: or other gitignore directories in the first place.
            - generic [ref=e253]:
              - heading "Why Git Subtrees Instead of Git Submodules?" [level=2] [ref=e254]
              - link "Why Git Subtrees Instead of Git Submodules?" [ref=e255] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#why-git-subtrees-instead-of-git-submodules"
                - img [ref=e257] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e259]:
              - text: It might seem intuitive to simply use
              - code [ref=e260]: git submodule
              - text: to vendor external software into a project. Submodules are (unfortunately) a familiar feature of
              - code [ref=e261]: git
              - text: for most developers, keep the repositories cleanly separated, and allow pinning external codebases to a specific commit.
            - paragraph [ref=e262]:
              - text: And to be honest, submodules are
              - emphasis [ref=e263]: fine
              - text: "- they accomplish the end goal which is allowing the agent direct access to external source code. But in our opinion there are features of"
              - code [ref=e264]: git submodule
              - text: that make them quite annoying for this specific purpose.
            - list [ref=e265]:
              - listitem [ref=e266]: They require explicit initialization when cloning a project, which (if you are like me) can be really easy to forget to do
              - listitem [ref=e267]: They also introduce a layer of indirection (your repo now “points” to another repo instead of just containing the source code)
              - listitem [ref=e268]:
                - text: They require additional metadata to be tracked in git (i.e. the
                - code [ref=e269]: .gitmodules
                - text: file)
            - paragraph [ref=e270]:
              - text: Instead,
              - code [ref=e271]: git subtree
              - text: allows you to directly nest one repository inside another as a subdirectory. Once a subtree is created, it behaves like any other directory in your project. That means both the engineers and coding agents working on a project can ignore the fact that a subtree is even in use.

tools.name: create
tools.description: Creates a new markdown document and adds it to the space.

tools.name: open
tools.description: Opens and reads the contents of a new markdown document.

The ID of the referenced object. Formats accepted:

- space ID, object ID tuple (spaceID:objectID)
- Only object ID that is assumed to be in the current space (01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4)

oldString.description: The text to find in the document.

newString.description: The text to replace it with.

replaceAll.description: If true, replaces all occurrences. Defaults to false (first occurrence only).

edits.description: The edits to apply to the document. Each edit finds oldString and replaces it with newString.

tools.name: load-object
tools.description:
Loads the object or relation content.
Can load multiple objects at at time.
Use the to read the data when you have a DXN.
Call this tool with an array of one or more DXNs or object IDs.
When use see a reference ({ '/': 'dxn:...' }), you can call this function to load the object.
Note that returned data is only a snapshot in time, and might have changed since the object was last loaded.

tools.name: create-object

Creates a new object and adds it to the current space.
Get the schema from the schema-list tool and ensure that the data matches the corresponding schema.
References are provided in the following format: { "/": "dxn:..." }.
Reference examples: { "/": "dxn:echo:@:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }, { "/": "dxn:queue:data:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4:01KG7R1ZXWFMWQ4DA1Q6TN1DG4" }

tools.name: query
tools.description:
Query for objects in ECHO.
Use this tool when searching for information in the space (both automerge and queues).
Currently, two types of queries are supported:
  - Full-text search - terms may appear anywhere in the object.
  - Type-based search - objects of a specific type.
You can use them together, for example, to search for objects of a specific type that match a full-text query.
Important: Whem querying by typename, make sure to list the schema first, to get the exact typename.

Omit both typename and text to search for all objects.

<output_format>
  You can choose to either return the full object data, or just the DXN, type and label.
  When expecting a lot of results, run with includeContent=false to not pollute the context, and then load specific objects using the load tool.

  You can choose to get the content right away, if you don't expect a lot of results.
  To load content right away, run with includeContent=true.
  When loading content, set an appropriate limit on the number of results to avoid overwhelming the context.
</output_format>

<example description="All tasks related to Cyberdyne and Bob">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.task",
    "text": "cyberdyne bob",
  }
</example>

<example description="Financial report Q1 2026">
  {
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.document",
    "text": "financial report Q1 2026",
    "includeContent": true
    "limit": 3
  }
</example>

<example description="Emails from specific mailboxes">
  {
    "in": [{"/" : "dxn:echo:@:YYYYYY"}, {"/" : "dxn:echo:@:XXXXXXX"}],
    "typename": "org.dxos.type.email",
    "includeContent": true,
    "limit": 20
  }
</example>

in.description: Scope the query to children of specific objects (transitively). Use this to query items within containers such as feeds or folders. For example, to find emails use `in: [mailbox1.feed, mailbox2.feed]`.

typename.description: The typename of the objects to list.

includeContent.description: Include the full object data in the response.

limit.description: The maximum number of results to return.

includeQueues.description: Search in queues as well as spaces. Only use this if searching for emails.

content.content:
## Blueprints Definitions

<blueprint>
  You are able to connect and use a virtual browser with persistent session.
  Browser tools are provided via MCP.
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can create, read and update markdown documents.
  When asked to edit or update documents return updates as a set of compact diff string pairs.
  For each diff, respond with the smallest possible matching span.
  
  Example:
  ```diff
  - "There is a tyop in this sentence."
  + "There is a typo in this sentence."
  - "This id good."
  + "This sentence is really good."
  ```
</blueprint>

<blueprint>
  You can query, create, update, and delete objects in ECHO.
  You can manage schemas, relations, tags, and add objects to the chat context.
</blueprint>

Scrape effect blog at https://effect.website/blog and find the content of last 3 articles.
          Create/update Person object for each author.
          Create Markdown document for each article.

content.text: Let me start by navigating to the Effect blog to scrape the articles, and also check what schemas are available for Person objects.
I'll start by navigating to the Effect blog and checking the available schemas for Person objects.

        - generic [ref=e213]:
          - generic [ref=e214]:
            - generic [ref=e215]:
              - time [ref=e217]: May 11, 2026
              - link "Maxwell Brown Maxwell Brown Founding Engineer" [ref=e219] [cursor=pointer]:

                - generic [ref=e221] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - generic [ref=e222] [cursor=pointer]: Maxwell Brown
                  - generic [ref=e223] [cursor=pointer]: Founding Engineer
            - paragraph [ref=e224]: One of the most common questions we get on the Effect team in the era of AI-assisted coding is how to get coding agents to write good, idiomatic Effect code.
            - paragraph [ref=e225]: "Our answer is always the same: give your agent real library code to learn from. More specifically, give the agent access to the Effect source code."
            - generic [ref=e226]:
              - heading "Stop Making Agents Guess" [level=2] [ref=e227]
              - link "Stop Making Agents Guess" [ref=e228] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#stop-making-agents-guess"
                - img [ref=e230] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e232]:
              - text: "If you follow us on social media, you’ve probably heard us recommend cloning the Effect repository directly into your project. The advice sounds strange until you remember what coding agents are actually good at: reading and exploring"
              - emphasis [ref=e233]: code
              - text: (crazy, I know).
            - paragraph [ref=e234]:
              - text: They’re substantially
              - emphasis [ref=e235]: less
              - text: "effective when working from documentation written for humans. When source code is available, an agent can explore it the way it was designed to: by following usage patterns, tracing abstractions, and learning from existing patterns. More importantly, this approach isn’t specific to Effect - it works for any external software your application depends on."
            - paragraph [ref=e236]: The most practical method for making external software explorable by your coding agent is to simply vendor the project’s Git repository directly into your application’s codebase.
            - paragraph [ref=e237]:
              - text: In this post we will explain why this works, why
              - code [ref=e238]: git subtrees
              - text: are a good fit for it, and how to set it up without making your project harder to manage.
            - generic [ref=e239]:
              - heading "Why Having the Source Code Available Matters" [level=2] [ref=e240]
              - link "Why Having the Source Code Available Matters" [ref=e241] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#why-having-the-source-code-available-matters"
                - img [ref=e243] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e245]: A common approach when utilizing coding agents is to rely on web search / web fetch to allow the agent to retrieve information about external dependencies when required. In practice, however, the agent will only have access to isolated code snippets without the surrounding structure, often requiring it to repeatedly fetch code it has already seen or until it has enough information to proceed. This is token inefficient and leads to highly fragmented context.
            - paragraph [ref=e246]:
              - text: Documentation has a different limitation - it usually explains what an API or library
              - emphasis [ref=e247]: does
              - text: but not how it is actually
              - emphasis [ref=e248]: used
              - text: across a codebase. Coding agents rely on patterns and examples, not descriptions.
            - paragraph [ref=e249]: Having source code available locally allows us to remedy both issues at once. When the agent is unsure about how to use a particular API or library, it can explore the actual implementation of that codebase just like any other part of your project.
            - paragraph [ref=e250]:
              - text: This is also why relying on code in
              - code [ref=e251]: node_modules
              - text: usually isn’t enough. The code is often compiled or flattened, which removes the structure that makes it useful to read. Even if a particular library does publish their uncompiled source, most coding agents are deoptimized from exploring
              - code [ref=e252]: node_modules
              - text: or other gitignore directories in the first place.
            - generic [ref=e253]:
              - heading "Why Git Subtrees Instead of Git Submodules?" [level=2] [ref=e254]
              - link "Why Git Subtrees Instead of Git Submodules?" [ref=e255] [cursor=pointer]:
                - /url: "#why-git-subtrees-instead-of-git-submodules"
                - img [ref=e257] [cursor=pointer]
            - paragraph [ref=e259]:
              - text: It might seem intuitive to simply use
              - code [ref=e260]: git submodule
              - text: to vendor external software into a project. Submodules are (unfortunately) a familiar feature of
              - code [ref=e261]: git
              - text: for most developers, keep the repositories cleanly separated, and allow pinning external codebases to a specific commit.
            - paragraph [ref=e262]:
              - text: And to be honest, submodules are
              - emphasis [ref=e263]: fine
              - text: "- they accomplish the end goal which is allowing the agent direct access to external source code. But in our opinion there are features of"
              - code [ref=e264]: git submodule
              - text: that make them quite annoying for this specific purpose.
            - list [ref=e265]:
              - listitem [ref=e266]: They require explicit initialization when cloning a project, which (if you are like me) can be really easy to forget to do

                - code [ref=e269]: .gitmodules

              - text: Instead,

              - text: allows you to directly nest one repository inside another as a subdirectory. Once a subtree is created, it behaves like any other directory in your project. That means both the engineers and coding agents working on a project can ignore the fact that a subtree is even in use.

            - paragraph [ref=e234]: Hi Effecters!
            - paragraph [ref=e235]:
              - text: Welcome back to This Week In Effect (
              - strong [ref=e236]: TWIE
              - text: ) - your weekly update of the latest developments in the Effect community and ecosystem.
            - paragraph [ref=e237]: Effect is a powerful TypeScript library that helps developers build complex, synchronous, and asynchronous programs. One key feature that sets Effect apart is how it leverages structured concurrency to provide features such as async cancellation and safe resource management, making it easier to build robust, scalable, and efficient programs.
            - paragraph [ref=e238]: To get started, below you’ll find links to our documentation as well as our guide for installing Effect. Enjoy!

            - paragraph [ref=e270]:
              - text: A steady week for the v4 beta, with new testing utilities, schema additions, and a handful of targeted bug fixes across HTTP, observability, and RPC — here are the most notable changes that landed in
              - code [ref=e271]: effect-smol
              - text: .
            - list [ref=e272]:
              - listitem [ref=e273]:
                - paragraph [ref=e274]:
                  - strong [ref=e275]: New APIs
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e276]: HttpApiTest
                  - text: module for testing HttpApi handlers without a running server, added
                  - code [ref=e277]: Effect.acquireDisposable
                  - text: for integrating with the TC39 Explicit Resource Management proposal (
                  - code [ref=e278]: using
                  - text: keyword), and ported
                  - code [ref=e279]: Effect.firstSuccessOf
                  - text: from v3.
              - listitem [ref=e280]:
                - paragraph [ref=e281]:
                  - strong [ref=e282]: Schema additions
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e283]: Schema.DurationFromString
                  - text: for parsing duration strings, and added
                  - code [ref=e284]: Config.literals
                  - text: as a convenience constructor for
                  - code [ref=e285]: Schema.Literals
                  - text: .
              - listitem [ref=e286]:
                - paragraph [ref=e287]:
                  - strong [ref=e288]: RPC
                  - text: ": Added"
                  - code [ref=e289]: Rpc.custom
                  - text: for defining custom RPC handlers with full control over the request/response lifecycle.
              - listitem [ref=e290]:
                - paragraph [ref=e291]:
                  - strong [ref=e292]: HTTP
                  - text: ": Fixed empty body decoding in"
                  - code [ref=e293]: HttpApiBuilder
                  - text: ", and fixed OpenAI config fields being incorrectly spread into the request body."
              - listitem [ref=e294]:
                - paragraph [ref=e295]:
                  - strong [ref=e296]: Observability & runtime
                  - text: ": Fixed OpenTelemetry logger severity mapping, moved the fiber lifetime metric start hook to the correct lifecycle point, and fixed"
                  - code [ref=e297]: formatJson
                  - text: circular reference handling while removing a duplicated
                  - code [ref=e298]: stringifyCircular
                  - text: utility.
              - listitem [ref=e299]:
                - paragraph [ref=e300]:
                  - strong [ref=e301]: Schema error messages
                  - text: ": Normalized"
                  - code [ref=e302]: SchemaTransformation
                  - text: decoding error messages for more consistent output.
              - listitem [ref=e303]:
                - paragraph [ref=e304]:
                  - strong [ref=e305]: Platform
                  - text: ": Allowed"
                  - code [ref=e306]: "null"
                  - text: for Kubernetes condition
                  - code [ref=e307]: lastTransitionTime
                  - text: to handle optional fields in K8s manifests.

            - paragraph [ref=e231]: Hi Effecters!
            - paragraph [ref=e232]:
              - text: Welcome back to This Week In Effect (
              - strong [ref=e233]: TWIE
              - text: ) - your weekly update of the latest developments in the Effect community and ecosystem.
            - paragraph [ref=e234]: Effect is a powerful TypeScript library that helps developers build complex, synchronous, and asynchronous programs. One key feature that sets Effect apart is how it leverages structured concurrency to provide features such as async cancellation and safe resource management, making it easier to build robust, scalable, and efficient programs.
            - paragraph [ref=e235]: To get started, below you’ll find links to our documentation as well as our guide for installing Effect. Enjoy!
            - list [ref=e236]:
              - listitem [ref=e237]:
                - link "Effect Documentation" [ref=e238] [cursor=pointer]:
                  - /url: /docs/getting-started/introduction/
              - listitem [ref=e239]:

                  - /url: /docs/getting-started/installation/

            - list [ref=e243]:

                - text: Release.
              - listitem [ref=e248]:
                - text: Durable workflows in TypeScript with
                - link "@effect/workflow" [ref=e249] [cursor=pointer]:

            - paragraph [ref=e267]:
              - text: Here are the most notable changes that landed in
              - code [ref=e268]: effect-smol
              - text: .
            - list [ref=e269]:
              - listitem [ref=e270]:
                - paragraph [ref=e271]:
                  - strong [ref=e272]: Reactivity
                  - text: ": Added an"
                  - code [ref=e273]: .exhaustive()
                  - text: finalizer to the
                  - code [ref=e274]: AsyncResult
                  - text: builder, ensuring all async result states are handled before the builder is sealed.
              - listitem [ref=e275]:
                - paragraph [ref=e276]:
                  - strong [ref=e277]: OpenAI & AI
                  - text: ": Added OpenAI reasoning support, expanding the AI integration capabilities introduced in recent weeks."
              - listitem [ref=e278]:
                - paragraph [ref=e279]:
                  - strong [ref=e280]: RPC & Entity
                  - text: ": Fixed the entity proxy RPC handler context, resolving an issue where the wrong context could be injected when using entity proxies."
              - listitem [ref=e281]:
                - paragraph [ref=e282]:
                  - strong [ref=e283]: IndexedDB
                  - text: ": Cleaned up"
                  - code [ref=e284]: IndexedDb
                  - text: prototypes, continuing the refinement of the IDB module.
              - listitem [ref=e285]:
                - paragraph [ref=e286]:
                  - strong [ref=e287]: Platform & internals
                  - text: ": Allowed"
                  - code [ref=e288]: Duration.Input
                  - text: with accessors for more ergonomic duration handling, and optimized binary array generation from streams to reduce unnecessary copying.
